title: A concurrent resolution expressing support for the Geneva Consensus Declaration on Promoting Women's Health and Strengthening the Family and urging that the United States rejoin this historic declaration.
plain_summary: This congressional resolution supports an international agreement called the Geneva Consensus Declaration that focuses on women's health and protecting families. The resolution wants the United States to rejoin this agreement that was signed by 39 countries and says there is no international right to abortion. Congress promises to work with the president to make sure the US does not pay for or support abortions in other countries. The resolution lets each country decide its own rules about women's health and family issues.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations.

sections.title: Title and Introduction
sections.text: This is a resolution that shows support for an agreement called the Geneva Consensus Declaration on Promoting Women's Health and Strengthening the Family. The resolution asks the United States to rejoin this agreement. Six senators introduced this resolution on January thirteenth, two thousand twenty five, and it was sent to the Committee on Foreign Relations to be reviewed.

sections.title: Background Statements
sections.text: The resolution explains several key points about the Geneva Consensus Declaration. The United States strongly supports women having the best possible health, life, dignity, and well-being throughout their lives. The Geneva Consensus Declaration was created by a diverse group of countries that wanted to improve women's health, protect families as the foundation of healthy societies, value life at all stages of development, and respect each country's right to make its own laws about these issues without outside pressure. Thirty two countries from around the world signed this declaration on October twenty second, two thousand twenty, representing more than one billion six hundred million people. Now thirty nine countries are part of this group. The United States was the main supporter of this declaration when Donald J. Trump was president. When Joseph R. Biden became president, he removed the United States from this agreement, but federal laws that stop the United States from doing or paying for abortions or forced family planning in other countries are still in place. The declaration states that all people are equal before the law and that women's human rights are an essential part of all human rights and freedoms. It says that every human being has dignity and worth and has the right to life. The declaration states that there is no international right to abortion and no requirement for countries to pay for or help with abortions. It also says that the family is the natural and basic unit of society and should be protected by society and the government. The declaration helps signatory countries work together so that no single country can be bullied or silenced about these core values.

sections.title: Congressional Resolution
sections.text: Congress resolves to take six specific actions. First, Congress supports the commitments made in the Geneva Consensus Declaration to improve women's health and protect life and families, and praises the countries that signed it for working to advance women's health, protect life at every stage while saying there is no international right to abortion, and support the importance of families in society. Second, Congress declares that the principles about women's health, the dignity of every life, and families recognized by the Geneva Consensus Declaration are always valid everywhere. Third, Congress welcomes chances to strengthen support for the Geneva Consensus Declaration. Fourth, Congress will defend every country's right to adopt national policies that promote women's health, protect the right to life, and strengthen families, as stated in the Geneva Consensus Declaration. Fifth, Congress will work with the executive branch (the president and federal agencies) to make sure the United States does not conduct or pay for abortions, abortion lobbying, or forced family planning in foreign countries, following longstanding federal law. Sixth, Congress urges the countries that signed the Geneva Consensus Declaration to defend the universal principles about valuing every life and supporting families that are expressed in the declaration.
